summary: O'Neil describes her new role as an unpaid consultant in the NYC Mayor's Office, working with social service agency data to help vulnerable populations access better services. She contrasts this work with her previous experience in finance and ad tech, where data subjects were abstracted as 'the market' or treated as prey. What strikes her most is the office's genuine engagement with data privacy ethics—conversations she's never encountered in the private sector unless she initiated them herself. The piece reflects on the tension between building effective predictive models and respecting the privacy of vulnerable populations.
key_points: The goal is to use data to help people (homeless, child services clients) access better services, with efficiency as a side effect rather than the primary aim
Linking more data about people improves behavioral prediction, but creates serious privacy tensions even when intentions are helpful
In finance, data is sanitized and abstracted as 'the market'; in ad tech, users are treated as prey—neither context prompted ethical conversations about data use
Working with vulnerable populations has prompted genuine discussions about data privacy ethics that O'Neil never experienced in private sector modeling work
money_quotes: Far too many data studies only care about efficiency – how to make do with fewer police or fewer ambulances – with no thought or care about whether the people experiencing the services are being affected.
I want to start with the people, and hope for efficiency gains, which I believe will come.
There's a tension between the best model and reasonable limits of privacy and decency, even when the model is intended to be used in a primarily helpful manner.

In all my time as a predictive modeler, I've never been in that kind of conversation, about the malicious things people could do with such-and-such profile information, or with this or that model, unless I started it myself.

When you work as a quant in finance, the data you work with is utterly sanitized to the point where, although it eventually trickles down to humans, you are asked to think of it as generated by some kind of machine, which we call 'the market.'

When you work in ad tech or other internet modeling, you think of users as the targets of your predatory goals: click on this, user, or buy that, user! They are prey, and the more we know about them the better our aim will be.
key_insight: The private sector treats data subjects as abstractions or targets, while public service work with vulnerable populations forces genuine reckoning with the ethics of prediction and privacy.
